text: Apple filed a patent infringement lawsuit against Samsung over touchscreen technology used in its Galaxy phones.
Google won its antitrust appeal against the European Commission, overturning a record 2.4 billion euro fine.
Meta was ordered by the Irish Data Protection Commission to pay a 1.2 billion euro fine for violating GDPR rules on data transfers.
Oracle lost its decade-long copyright battle against Google over the use of Java APIs in Android.
The FTC alleged that Microsoft engaged in anticompetitive practices by bundling Teams with its Office 365 suite.
Qualcomm was subpoenaed by the DOJ as part of a broader investigation into chip licensing practices across the semiconductor industry.
Amazon acquired MGM Studios for 8.45 billion dollars, gaining a vast library of films and television content.
Nvidia and TSMC announced a deepened partnership to co-develop next-generation Blackwell GPU fabrication at the 3nm process node.
Intel lost its contract to supply processors for Apple's Mac lineup as Apple transitioned to its own in-house silicon chips.
Apple canceled its decade-long autonomous vehicle project, redirecting thousands of engineers toward generative AI initiatives.
SoftBank agreed to acquire Ampere Computing, a cloud-native chip designer, for 6.5 billion dollars.
Ford and SK Innovation announced a joint venture to build three battery manufacturing plants across the United States.
Tesla announced plans to build a new Gigafactory in Mexico, its first major manufacturing facility outside the United States and China.
Pfizer reported that its mRNA cancer vaccine candidate achieved a 44 percent reduction in recurrence in late-stage trials.
Tesla delivered 435,000 vehicles in Q3, missing analyst consensus of 463,000 and marking the second consecutive quarter of declining volumes.
Nvidia reported revenue of 22.1 billion dollars, exceeding Wall Street estimates of 20.4 billion dollars on surging data center demand.
Intel reported earnings in line with analyst expectations, with data center revenue flat and PC segment showing modest recovery.
Google hired more than 30 senior AI researchers from OpenAI, offering compensation packages reportedly exceeding 10 million dollars.
Apple's chief design officer Jony Ive resigned to establish his own independent creative firm.
Microsoft laid off 10,000 employees, roughly 5 percent of its global workforce, citing slowing cloud growth and economic uncertainty.
Amazon Web Services lost its lead cloud contract with the US Department of Defense to Microsoft Azure in a reversal of an earlier award.
Novo Nordisk received FDA approval for its obesity drug Wegovy for cardiovascular risk reduction, expanding its approved patient population significantly.
Boeing's 737 MAX was grounded globally by aviation regulators following two fatal crashes linked to software defects in the MCAS system.
TSMC's production delays at its 3nm node forced Apple to push back its iPhone 16 Pro launch by approximately six weeks.
The US government imposed export restrictions on advanced AI chips, barring Nvidia from selling its H100 and A100 GPUs to Chinese customers.
Apple lost several top AI researchers to Google and Meta amid its strict return-to-office policy at its Cupertino headquarters.
Samsung filed for bankruptcy protection for its display panel subsidiary amid collapsing LCD prices and overcapacity in the market.
General Motors emerged from bankruptcy restructuring ahead of schedule, raising 23 billion dollars in its IPO.
OpenAI launched GPT-5, claiming a 40 percent improvement in reasoning benchmarks over its predecessor.
Qualcomm was granted a patent for a new low-power 5G modem architecture that outperforms existing designs by a factor of three.
Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk both reported stronger than expected sales of their GLP-1 drugs, with combined revenue surpassing analyst forecasts by 15 percent.
New Basel IV capital requirements forced major European banks including Deutsche Bank and BNP Paribas to raise additional tier-one capital buffers.
Waymo, Alphabet's autonomous vehicle subsidiary, launched a paid robotaxi service in San Francisco following regulatory approval.
ASML reported record order intake of 9 billion euros for its next-generation EUV lithography machines, driven by demand from TSMC and Samsung.
Broadcom completed its acquisition of VMware for 69 billion dollars after receiving final regulatory approval from the European Union.
Arm Holdings priced its IPO at 51 dollars per share, raising 4.87 billion dollars in the largest technology listing of the year.
Johnson and Johnson agreed to pay 8.9 billion dollars to settle tens of thousands of lawsuits alleging its talc-based baby powder caused cancer.
Alphabet announced a 70 billion dollar share buyback program and initiated its first ever quarterly dividend of 20 cents per share.
Rivian secured a 5 billion dollar investment from Volkswagen in exchange for a joint venture to co-develop next-generation EV software platforms.
TSMC warned that geopolitical tensions over Taiwan could disrupt its chip supply to global customers including Apple, Nvidia, and AMD.
Stripe poached PayPal's head of enterprise sales along with a team of five senior account executives.
OpenAI's chief scientist Ilya Sutskever resigned to found his own AI safety startup.
Meta hired more than 100 engineers from Twitter following Elon Musk's mass layoffs at the social media platform.
Goldman Sachs promoted a record number of managing directors this cycle, signaling confidence in its dealmaking pipeline.
Boeing's CEO Dave Calhoun announced his resignation amid ongoing safety and production crises at the aircraft manufacturer.
Salesforce lost its co-CEO Bret Taylor to a new venture, leaving Marc Benioff as sole chief executive.
Apple rehired several former chip engineers from Intel's failed mobile division to bolster its silicon design team.
Zoom laid off 1,300 employees, roughly 15 percent of its workforce, as pandemic-era growth reversed sharply.
Google DeepMind unveiled Gemini Ultra, claiming it surpassed GPT-4 on all major academic benchmarks.
Samsung launched its Galaxy S25 lineup featuring its first fully in-house developed mobile processor.
Microsoft released Copilot for Microsoft 365, integrating GPT-4 across Word, Excel, and Teams for enterprise customers.
Tesla unveiled its long-awaited Robotaxi, a fully autonomous vehicle with no steering wheel or pedals.
Anthropic released Claude 3 Opus, which outperformed GPT-4 on reasoning and coding benchmarks according to internal evaluations.
Rivian recalled all 13,000 of its delivered vehicles due to a loose fastener that could cause drivers to lose steering control.
AMD patented a new chiplet interconnect architecture that significantly reduces latency between compute dies.
Bed Bath and Beyond filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection after failing to secure additional financing from lenders.
WeWork filed for bankruptcy, marking a dramatic collapse for a company once valued at 47 billion dollars.
Hertz emerged from bankruptcy with a restructured balance sheet and a new fleet strategy focused on electric vehicles.
Celsius Network filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy, freezing withdrawals for more than 1.7 million customers.
Sears emerged from bankruptcy for the second time in three years, raising doubts about the viability of its turnaround plan.
Apple's App Store generated 1.1 trillion dollars in developer billings and sales in 2023, according to an independent study commissioned by Apple.
Snap reported a 39 percent decline in revenue as advertisers shifted budgets toward TikTok and Instagram Reels.
TSMC reported record quarterly profit of 9.2 billion dollars, driven by surging demand for AI chips from Nvidia and Apple.
Disney's streaming segment turned profitable for the first time, offsetting continued weakness in its linear television business.
Alibaba missed revenue estimates for the third consecutive quarter as domestic consumption in China remained sluggish.
JPMorgan Chase reported record full-year profit of 49.6 billion dollars, driven by higher interest rates boosting net interest income.
Airbnb reported its first ever full-year profit, generating 1.9 billion dollars in net income on revenue of 8.4 billion dollars.
Credit Suisse reported a full-year loss of 7.3 billion Swiss francs as client outflows accelerated amid confidence crisis.
The DOJ indicted former Theranos CEO Elizabeth Holmes on federal wire fraud charges related to deception of investors and patients.
Binance pleaded guilty to money laundering violations and agreed to pay 4.3 billion dollars in penalties to US regulators.
Epic Games won its antitrust case against Google, with a jury finding that Google illegally monopolized the Android app distribution market.
The SEC charged Coinbase with operating an unregistered securities exchange, threatening its core business model.
Exxon Mobil won its lawsuit against activist shareholders who had pushed for binding climate resolutions at its annual meeting.
Apple and Google were both named in a class action lawsuit alleging their app store duopoly artificially inflated prices for consumers.
Pfizer acquired Seagen for 43 billion dollars, gaining access to its antibody-drug conjugate cancer therapy pipeline.
Disney sold its stake in Vice Media as part of a broader retreat from digital media investments made during the streaming boom.
Microsoft and Mistral AI announced a partnership giving Microsoft exclusive rights to distribute Mistral's large language models via Azure.
Sony acquired Bungie, the game studio behind Destiny, for 3.6 billion dollars to bolster its PlayStation live service portfolio.
Amazon shut down its Amazon Care telehealth service just two years after launching it to employees and external customers.
Volkswagen announced it would close three factories in Germany and cut tens of thousands of jobs as EV demand fell short of targets.
Netflix and Microsoft announced a partnership to launch an ad-supported subscription tier, with Microsoft handling ad sales technology.
Qualcomm acquired Nuvia, a chip startup founded by former Apple silicon engineers, for 1.4 billion dollars.
Google terminated its contract with a defense contractor after employee protests over Project Nimbus, an AI contract with the Israeli government.
Lyft sold its autonomous vehicle division Level 5 to Toyota's Woven Planet subsidiary for 550 million dollars.

Also weighing on markets was concern over Nvidia's export curbs. Its H100 chips are now banned from sale in China, hitting its data center revenue outlook, while AMD said its MI300 sales could also be affected by the restrictions.

Investors cheered as Microsoft reported strong Azure growth of 29 percent. But its shares gave back some gains after the company warned that capital expenditure would rise significantly in the coming quarters to fund AI infrastructure.
Apple's iPhone sales in China fell 19 percent in the first quarter as Huawei's Mate 60 series gained significant market share following its surprise launch.
Shares of ASML dropped sharply after the Dutch government revoked its export license for chipmaking equipment to China, following pressure from the US.
SoftBank's Vision Fund posted a 32 billion dollar loss for the fiscal year, its worst performance since inception, dragged down by writedowns on WeWork and other portfolio companies.

Shares of Eli Lilly surged after its experimental Alzheimer's drug donanemab significantly slowed cognitive decline in a late-stage trial, while rival Biogen's shares fell as investors rotated out of its approved treatment Leqembi.
JPMorgan agreed to acquire First Republic Bank after US regulators seized the failed lender, marking the second largest bank failure in American history.
UBS was forced by Swiss regulators to acquire Credit Suisse for 3 billion Swiss francs in an emergency rescue deal over a single weekend.
Broadcom shares rose after it reported that its AI chip revenue had tripled year over year, driven by custom silicon orders from Google and Meta.
Spotify announced it was raising subscription prices for the second time in a year across all markets, citing rising content and royalty costs.
Tesla cut prices on its entire vehicle lineup for the fourth time this year, raising fears of a broader EV price war that could hurt margins across the industry.
ARM Holdings saw its shares double within days of its Nasdaq IPO, making it the most successful semiconductor listing in over a decade.
Instacart priced its IPO below its initial target range amid weak investor appetite, raising 660 million dollars at a valuation far below its pandemic-era peak.
AMD's shares fell after it announced the acquisition of server maker ZT Systems for 4.9 billion dollars, with analysts questioning whether the price was justified.
Xiaomi received regulatory approval to begin selling its first electric vehicle in China, entering a market dominated by BYD and Tesla.
Concerns mounted over the semiconductor supply chain after an earthquake in Taiwan disrupted operations at several TSMC facilities, sending shares of Apple, Nvidia, and AMD lower.
Bank of America reported stronger than expected earnings, with net interest income rising 8 percent as the lender benefited from higher rates.
Johnson and Johnson lost its appeal against a 2.1 billion dollar talc verdict, dealing a major blow to the healthcare giant's legal strategy.
General Motors announced it would halt production at its Orion Assembly plant in Michigan, citing weak demand for its electric pickup trucks.
Berkshire Hathaway increased its stake in Occidental Petroleum to 28 percent, fueling speculation that Warren Buffett may pursue a full takeover.
Morgan Stanley upgraded its outlook for Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company, citing accelerating orders from artificial intelligence chip designers.
The New York Times filed a copyright lawsuit against Microsoft and OpenAI, alleging that millions of its articles were used to train large language models without permission.
Wells Fargo agreed to pay 3.7 billion dollars to settle charges by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau over widespread mismanagement of customer accounts.
Procter and Gamble raised its full-year earnings forecast after posting better than expected quarterly results driven by price increases across its consumer brands.
Lockheed Martin won a 17 billion dollar contract from the US Department of Defense to supply F-35 fighter jets over the next five years.
Bristol Myers Squibb acquired Karuna Therapeutics for 14 billion dollars, gaining access to its pipeline of neuropsychiatric drug candidates.
The chipmaker posted record quarterly sales, driven by insatiable demand for its artificial intelligence accelerators from cloud computing giants.
The e-commerce giant slashed its workforce by 18,000 employees, the largest layoff in the company's history, as it pulled back from pandemic-era expansion.
The Cupertino company faced fresh regulatory scrutiny in Europe after the bloc ruled that its App Store payment practices violated digital markets rules.
The Redmond software giant reported that its cloud division grew 29 percent year over year, beating analyst estimates for the sixth consecutive quarter.
The electric vehicle maker cut prices on its best-selling sedan by 2,000 dollars, intensifying a price war with legacy automakers entering the EV market.
The search giant agreed to pay 1.4 billion dollars to settle biometric data privacy claims brought by the state of Texas.
The social media company announced it would begin charging users eight dollars per month for its verification badge, reversing years of offering the feature for free.
Advanced Micro Devices reported its data center revenue surpassed 2.3 billion dollars for the first time, driven by strong adoption of its AI accelerator chips.
International Business Machines beat earnings estimates as its consulting division returned to growth following a period of client spending caution.
Taiwan Semiconductor warned that geopolitical risks could force it to accelerate overseas factory construction at significantly higher cost than domestic operations.
JPMorgan's Jamie Dimon warned of stagflation risks in his annual shareholder letter, sending shares of major US banks lower.
Elon Musk's acquisition of Twitter, later rebranded as X, was completed at 44 billion dollars, a price widely seen as significantly above market value.
Sam Altman was reinstated as OpenAI's chief executive just days after being abruptly fired by the board, following a revolt by staff and investors.
Nvidia's Jensen Huang unveiled the company's next-generation Blackwell architecture at GTC, sending the stock up 7 percent in after-hours trading.
Apple's Tim Cook testified before Congress defending the company's App Store policies, denying that Apple held a monopoly over iPhone software distribution.
BofA upgraded Tesla to buy from neutral, citing improving margins and the upcoming launch of its affordable vehicle model.
MS raised its price target on NVDA to 1,100 dollars, calling it the primary beneficiary of the AI infrastructure spending cycle.
GOOG shares fell after reports emerged that Apple was considering replacing its default search engine with an AI-powered alternative.
TSLA delivered fewer vehicles than expected in Q2, missing the Street by 5 percent, while BYD overtook it as the world's top EV seller.
AMZN beat on both revenue and earnings per share, with AWS growing 17 percent year over year and advertising revenue accelerating to 12.1 billion dollars.
Shares of the Dutch chip equipment maker fell sharply after Washington pressured the Netherlands to restrict exports of its most advanced lithography machines to China.
The Japanese automaker recalled 1.4 million vehicles in North America over a defect in the fuel pump that could cause sudden engine stalls.
The South Korean battery maker secured a 7 billion dollar supply agreement with a major US electric vehicle manufacturer to supply next-generation lithium iron phosphate cells.

extractions.entity: a major US electric vehicle manufacturer

text: The German automaker said it would invest 30 billion euros in electric vehicle development over the next five years, doubling its previous commitment.
The streaming giant lost 1.2 million subscribers in North America, its first quarterly decline in over a decade, sending its shares down 35 percent.
The ride-hailing company posted its first ever quarterly profit on an adjusted basis, a milestone that sent its shares up 9 percent in after-hours trading.
Also under pressure was the payments processor, whose shares dropped after it revealed that fraud losses had exceeded internal targets for the third consecutive quarter.
The drugmaker's shares surged after its experimental weight loss pill showed results comparable to injectable GLP-1 therapies in a mid-stage clinical trial.
Shares of the semiconductor equipment company dropped after its largest customer announced plans to delay its next fabrication plant by eighteen months.
The Chinese tech giant was added to the US Entity List, barring American companies from supplying it with components or software without a government license.
Shares of Moderna fell after its rival reported positive Phase 3 data for an mRNA flu vaccine, raising fears of increased competition in the respiratory vaccine market.
Palantir won a 480 million dollar contract from the US Army to expand its battlefield AI software across active combat units.
Block, formerly known as Square, reported that its Cash App monthly active users declined for the first time since the product launched.
Meta Platforms, the parent company of Facebook and Instagram, reported advertising revenue grew 27 percent as its AI-driven feed recommendations improved engagement.
Alphabet Inc posted revenue of 86.3 billion dollars, beating consensus by 3 percent, as Google Search held its dominant position despite rising competition from AI chatbots.
NVidia, the AI chip darling, saw its market capitalization briefly surpass three trillion dollars, overtaking Microsoft as the world's most valuable company.
Apple Inc's services revenue reached an all-time high of 23.9 billion dollars, growing 14 percent year over year and accounting for a rising share of total company profits.
Amazon.com reported that its advertising business surpassed 14 billion dollars in quarterly revenue for the first time, growing faster than both Google and Meta's ad units.
Shares of ASML Holding fell after management warned that customer order cancellations in China could reduce its 2024 revenue guidance by up to 10 percent.
Novo Nordisk A/S overtook LVMH as Europe's most valuable listed company, driven by surging demand for its obesity and diabetes treatments.
The maker of the world's most advanced chip manufacturing equipment said customer demand remained robust despite ongoing trade restrictions between the US and China.

extractions.entity: the maker of the world's most advanced chip manufacturing equipment

text: Buffett's conglomerate disclosed a new stake in a major Japanese trading house, sending the latter's shares to a multi-decade high in Tokyo trading.
Musk's rocket company successfully landed its Super Heavy booster for the first time, a milestone that analysts said could dramatically reduce launch costs.
Cook's company was fined 1.8 billion euros by European regulators for restricting music streaming rivals from informing users about cheaper subscription options.
Zuckerberg's social network reported its daily active users crossed three billion for the first time, cementing its position as the world's largest social platform.
The iPhone maker and the search giant jointly face an antitrust probe over their revenue sharing agreement that makes Google the default search engine on Apple devices.
The chip designer and the foundry announced they would jointly develop a custom server processor for a major hyperscaler, combining design expertise with leading-edge manufacturing.
The ride-hailing giant and the food delivery company announced a merger that would create a combined entity valued at over 50 billion dollars.
The biotech and the pharmaceutical giant announced positive Phase 3 results for their jointly developed cancer immunotherapy, sending both companies' shares sharply higher.
The payment network and the regional bank terminated their co-branded credit card partnership after a decade, with the bank switching to a competing card network.
Nvidia and AMD both warned that new US export restrictions on AI chips would materially impact their China revenue in the coming quarters.
Apple and Google face a landmark antitrust trial over their search default agreement, with the DOJ arguing the deal illegally entrenched Google's dominance.
Tesla and BYD both reported record quarterly deliveries, signaling that global EV demand remained robust despite macroeconomic headwinds.
Microsoft and Google each announced billion-dollar AI infrastructure investments in Southeast Asia on the same day, intensifying their rivalry for cloud market share in the region.
Pfizer and Moderna both reported declining revenue as demand for their Covid vaccines continued to normalize following the end of the pandemic emergency.
Samsung and SK Hynix reported sharply higher memory chip profits as AI server demand drove a sustained recovery in DRAM and NAND pricing.
JPMorgan and Goldman Sachs both posted record investment banking fees as the mergers and acquisitions market rebounded strongly from a two-year drought.
Airbus overtook Boeing as the world's largest aircraft manufacturer by deliveries for the fifth consecutive year, as Boeing continued to struggle with production and safety issues.
Spotify gained subscribers at the expense of Apple Music and Amazon Music as its AI-powered playlist feature drove a surge in user engagement.
Visa and Mastercard agreed to cap interchange fees for five years as part of a 30 billion dollar settlement with US merchants.
Intel lost market share to both AMD and Arm-based processors in the server market, with its data center division posting its third consecutive quarterly decline.
Netflix gained subscribers in every region while Disney Plus and Paramount Plus both reported net subscriber losses in their latest quarterly results.
TSMC raised its capital expenditure forecast while Samsung cut its chip investment budget, widening the technology gap between the two foundries.
Uber gained significant market share from Lyft in major US cities after launching its new loyalty program, with Lyft acknowledging the competitive pressure in its earnings call.
Shell and BP both reported weaker earnings as oil prices fell below 80 dollars a barrel, while TotalEnergies outperformed peers on stronger refining margins.
Amazon Web Services lost cloud market share to Microsoft Azure for the second consecutive quarter, according to data from Synergy Research Group.
The semiconductor giant reported that its foundry division continued to bleed cash, losing 7 billion dollars in the first half of the year as it struggled to attract customers away from TSMC.
The luxury goods conglomerate reported slowing sales in China as consumer confidence weakened, while its rival posted double-digit growth in the same market.
The activist hedge fund disclosed a 1.9 billion dollar stake in the consumer goods company and immediately called for the spin-off of its underperforming personal care division.
The index provider announced it would remove the Chinese property developer from its benchmark indices following the company's failure to make coupon payments on offshore bonds.
Concerns over Apple's growth outlook deepened after its largest contract manufacturer warned of weaker than expected orders for the upcoming iPhone cycle.
Nvidia's dominance in AI training chips came under threat after Google announced its sixth-generation tensor processing unit delivered three times the performance per watt.
Tesla's stock fell sharply after a fatal accident involving its Full Self-Driving software triggered a federal investigation by the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration.
Meta's advertising revenue recovery surprised analysts as its Advantage Plus AI ad targeting system significantly outperformed manual campaign optimization for retail clients.
Amazon's pharmacy business gained ground on CVS Health and Walgreens after it expanded same-day prescription delivery to 20 additional US cities.
Salesforce shares dropped after its chief operating officer resigned unexpectedly, the third C-suite departure at the software company in eighteen months.
OpenAI hired Fidji Simo, former CEO of Instacart, as its new head of applications, signaling a push toward commercializing its consumer products.
Anthropic appointed a former Google DeepMind research director as its new chief scientist, strengthening its technical leadership ahead of a major funding round.
Disney named a former Netflix executive as president of its streaming division, tasked with reversing subscriber losses and improving content return on investment.
Citigroup laid off 20,000 employees as part of its largest restructuring in decades under new CEO Jane Fraser, who pledged to simplify the bank's complex organizational structure.
PayPal hired Alex Chriss from Intuit as its new chief executive, replacing Dan Schulman who retired after a decade leading the payments company.
The Federal Reserve fined Deutsche Bank 186 million dollars for failing to fix longstanding deficiencies in its anti-money laundering controls.
The European Commission approved Microsoft's acquisition of Activision Blizzard subject to conditions, overruling the UK's Competition and Markets Authority which had blocked the deal.
Binance founder Changpeng Zhao was sentenced to four months in prison after pleading guilty to money laundering charges, as part of a 4.3 billion dollar settlement with the DOJ.
The SEC dropped its lawsuit against Ripple Labs, a partial victory for the crypto company which had fought the regulator for four years over whether XRP was an unregistered security.
Boeing's 737 MAX 9 was grounded by the FAA after a door plug blew out mid-flight on an Alaska Airlines jet, exposing a manufacturing quality crisis at the planemaker.
Alphabet's self-driving unit Waymo was cleared by California regulators to expand its paid robotaxi service to all of San Francisco, dealing a blow to rival Cruise which had its license suspended.
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company announced it would build a second chip factory in Arizona after receiving 6.6 billion dollars in grants from the US CHIPS Act.
Intel received 8.5 billion dollars in direct funding under the CHIPS Act to build semiconductor fabrication plants in Ohio, Arizona, and New Mexico.
SoftBank's Vision Fund posted its first quarterly profit in two years as the valuations of its technology portfolio companies recovered alongside rising public markets.
Uber Technologies reported its first ever annual operating profit, a milestone that chief executive Dara Khosrowshahi called a turning point for the company.
Peloton reported its eighth consecutive quarterly revenue decline as pandemic-era demand for its connected fitness equipment continued to unwind.
Block reported that Cash App gross profit grew 21 percent year over year, offsetting weakness in its Square merchant services division.
Shopify beat revenue estimates after its merchant solutions segment accelerated, helped by market share gains from WooCommerce and Magento.
Palantir's commercial revenue grew 55 percent driven by demand for its AIP artificial intelligence platform, with the company raising full-year guidance for the third consecutive quarter.
Snowflake disappointed investors with weaker than expected product revenue growth, as competition from Databricks and cloud-native data warehouses intensified.
Arm Holdings reported royalty revenue grew 37 percent as its v9 architecture gained rapid adoption among smartphone and data center chip designers.
Qualcomm beat earnings estimates and raised guidance after securing new design wins at Samsung for its Snapdragon processors, reversing earlier losses to Samsung's in-house Exynos chips.
Texas Instruments warned of a prolonged semiconductor downturn in industrial and automotive markets, sending shares of peers including Microchip Technology and ON Semiconductor sharply lower.
Applied Materials reported record backlog as chipmakers accelerated investment in advanced packaging technology to meet AI chip demand.
Micron Technology forecast stronger than expected revenue for the next quarter, driven by high bandwidth memory orders from Nvidia for its Blackwell GPU systems.
Western Digital and Seagate both cut their quarterly dividends as hard disk drive demand collapsed faster than anticipated due to the shift to solid state storage.
Lam Research warned that export controls on semiconductor equipment sales to China would reduce its annual revenue by approximately 2 billion dollars.
KLA Corporation raised its dividend and announced a 2 billion dollar share buyback after posting record quarterly earnings from its process control equipment division.
Broadcom's VMware integration exceeded management expectations, with cross-selling of networking software to existing VMware customers accelerating faster than the acquisition model assumed.
Workday lost its largest federal government contract to Oracle after a competitive rebid, representing approximately 300 million dollars in annual recurring revenue.
ServiceNow raised full-year guidance after its Now Assist AI features drove faster enterprise deal closures and higher average contract values than the prior year.
CrowdStrike faced significant customer backlash and potential legal liability after a faulty software update caused a global IT outage affecting 8.5 million Windows devices.
Palo Alto Networks cut its billings forecast, triggering a broad selloff in cybersecurity stocks including CrowdStrike, Fortinet, and Zscaler.
Cloudflare signed its largest ever enterprise contract, a multi-year deal with a major financial institution worth over 100 million dollars.
Johnson Controls agreed to sell its residential and light commercial HVAC business to Bosch for 8.1 billion dollars as part of a broader portfolio simplification strategy.
Honeywell International announced it would spin off its advanced materials division into a separate publicly traded company to unlock shareholder value.
General Electric completed its three-way breakup, with GE Aerospace, GE Vernova, and GE HealthCare all trading as independent companies.
3M agreed to pay 10.3 billion dollars to settle claims that its Combat Arms earplugs caused hearing damage to hundreds of thousands of US military veterans.
Caterpillar beat earnings estimates as infrastructure spending in North America and the Middle East offset weakness in its mining equipment segment in China.
Deere and Company cut its full-year profit forecast for the second time as farmers delayed equipment purchases amid falling crop prices and high interest rates.
United Parcel Service lost its largest customer FedEx to a rival logistics network, costing it approximately 1.5 billion dollars in annual revenue.
Delta Air Lines upgraded its full-year profit outlook as business travel demand recovered to pre-pandemic levels and fuel costs declined more than expected.
Southwest Airlines faced pressure from activist investor Elliott Management, which disclosed a 1.9 billion dollar stake and demanded a change in leadership.
American Airlines cut its revenue forecast after its decision to reduce reliance on travel agents backfired, causing a sharp drop in corporate bookings.
Novo Nordisk's obesity drug Wegovy faced a new challenger as Eli Lilly's tirzepatide showed superior weight loss results in a head-to-head clinical trial.
AstraZeneca voluntarily withdrew its Covid vaccine from global markets, citing a rare side effect that caused blood clotting in a small number of recipients.
Merck won a key patent dispute against Moderna over mRNA technology used in Covid vaccines, potentially entitling it to royalties on billions of dollars in future sales.
AbbVie acquired ImmunoGen for 10.1 billion dollars to bolster its oncology pipeline ahead of the expiration of its blockbuster drug Humira's patent protection.
Regeneron and Sanofi reported that their jointly developed drug Dupixent crossed 10 billion dollars in annual sales for the first time, becoming one of the world's best-selling medicines.
Exxon Mobil completed its 60 billion dollar acquisition of Pioneer Natural Resources, creating the largest oil producer in the Permian Basin.
Chevron's acquisition of Hess was delayed after Exxon Mobil filed for arbitration claiming a right of first refusal over Hess's stake in a lucrative Guyana oilfield.
Saudi Aramco reported a 25 percent drop in annual profit as OPEC production cuts reduced its output while global oil prices remained under pressure.
NextEra Energy raised its long-term earnings growth forecast after securing a record number of power purchase agreements for its renewable energy projects.
Enphase Energy warned of a sharp revenue decline as rooftop solar installations slowed dramatically following the rollback of California's net metering policy.

Shares of First Solar surged after the Treasury Department confirmed that its domestically manufactured panels would qualify for the full 30 percent investment tax credit under the Inflation Reduction Act.
Rivian and Lucid Motors both warned they could run out of cash within twelve months unless they secured additional financing, rattling investors in the EV startup sector.
Li Auto reported record quarterly deliveries, overtaking Nio and Xpeng to become China's best-selling premium electric vehicle brand.
Volkswagen's Porsche division reported its operating margin fell below 15 percent for the first time in five years, missing the brand's long-held profitability target.
Stellantis warned of a sharp profit decline after its North American dealers reported record inventory levels, forcing the automaker to offer significant incentives to clear unsold vehicles.
Hyundai Motor and Kia reported combined global sales surpassed those of Volkswagen Group for the first time, marking a historic shift in the global automotive rankings.
Target's quarterly profit collapsed after it was forced to write down 1.5 billion dollars of excess inventory accumulated during a post-pandemic demand miscalculation.
Costco reported same-store sales growth of 6.5 percent, outperforming both Walmart and Target as its membership model continued to attract value-conscious consumers.
Dollar General cut its full-year outlook as its core low-income customer base reduced discretionary spending amid persistent inflation and depleted savings.
Estee Lauder cut its dividend and replaced its chief executive after organic sales fell for the sixth consecutive quarter, driven by weak demand in Asia travel retail.
Nike lost a key endorsement deal with basketball star Ja Morant to rival Puma following a high-profile conduct controversy involving the athlete.
Adidas reported a return to profitability after successfully selling off most of its remaining Yeezy inventory following the termination of its partnership with Ye.
Lululemon missed revenue estimates and cut guidance after its chief product officer resigned, raising concerns about slowing innovation in its core women's apparel category.
Marriott International raised its full-year revenue per available room forecast as leisure travel demand remained resilient and business travel continued its recovery.
Hilton Worldwide reported record hotel occupancy rates globally while Hyatt Hotels warned of softening demand in its luxury resort segment.
Booking Holdings gained market share from Expedia as its Genius loyalty program drove stronger customer retention and repeat booking rates.
Warner Bros Discovery reported a 9.1 billion dollar goodwill impairment charge related to its linear television networks, reflecting the accelerating decline of traditional cable.
Comcast lost more video subscribers than any quarter in its history as cord-cutting accelerated, though its broadband and theme park divisions partially offset the decline.
T-Mobile gained postpaid phone subscribers for the thirteenth consecutive quarter at the expense of AT&T and Verizon, widening its lead as the fastest-growing US wireless carrier.
Charter Communications and Cox Communications announced a merger to create the largest US cable operator by subscribers, challenging Comcast's market leadership.
Also weighing on semiconductor stocks was news that TSMC had quietly reduced orders from ASML for its next-generation high-NA EUV machines, suggesting a softer than expected demand outlook.
Shares of Arm rallied after Apple confirmed it would use Arm's latest v9 architecture in its next generation of Mac processors, while Intel's share of the laptop market fell to a record low.

The enterprise software company beat earnings but its stock fell after management warned that customers were taking longer to sign new contracts due to budget scrutiny, while its smaller rival reported accelerating growth.
The Chinese EV maker reported its first monthly sales figure exceeding 50,000 units, overtaking both its domestic rivals and the American incumbent in the premium segment.
The activist investor disclosed a stake in the media conglomerate and publicly called for the separation of its studio and streaming assets from its legacy broadcast and cable networks.
The online travel platform reported record gross bookings as its marketing efficiency improved, taking share from its larger rival which had cut its advertising budget.
The cloud database provider lost its co-founder and long-serving chief executive to retirement, triggering a sharp share price decline as investors worried about strategic continuity.
The fast food chain reported its worst same-store sales decline in a decade after a food safety incident linked to its supply chain triggered a nationwide consumer boycott.

The Japanese semiconductor company announced a partnership with a US chip designer to develop next-generation power management chips for electric vehicles, challenging Texas Instruments in the automotive market.
The South Korean conglomerate announced it would invest 300 billion dollars in semiconductors, displays, and biopharmaceuticals over the next five years, its largest ever capital commitment.
Nvidia's CEO Jensen Huang announced a 500 billion dollar AI infrastructure investment commitment in the United States, made alongside announcements from Microsoft, Google, and SoftBank.

DeepSeek's release of its R1 model, which matched GPT-4 performance at a fraction of the training cost, sent shares of Nvidia down 17 percent and triggered a broad selloff in AI infrastructure stocks.

OpenAI launched its o3 reasoning model, which achieved human-level performance on several graduate-level scientific benchmarks, prompting Anthropic and Google to accelerate their own reasoning model releases.
Meta released Llama 3, its most capable open-source large language model to date, putting pressure on commercial API providers including OpenAI and Anthropic as enterprises gained a free alternative.
xAI launched Grok 2, claiming it outperformed Claude and Gemini on coding benchmarks, intensifying competition in the rapidly crowding AI assistant market.

Mistral AI released a new mixture-of-experts model that outperformed Llama on several European language benchmarks, strengthening the French startup's position as the leading open-source AI company in Europe.

The AI startup's valuation was cut in half during its latest funding round as investors grew skeptical of its path to profitability and questioned whether its technology was sufficiently differentiated.
The venture capital firm led a 1 billion dollar funding round for the robotics company, which had recently signed supply agreements with three of the world's largest automotive manufacturers.
The quantum computing startup achieved a breakthrough that its scientists claimed solved a problem that would take classical supercomputers millions of years, sending its stock to an all-time high.
The fintech lender filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after rising interest rates caused its cost of capital to exceed the yields on its loan portfolio for three consecutive quarters.

Also notable was the performance divergence in cloud stocks: while the hyperscalers beat estimates, mid-tier providers including Rackspace and DigitalOcean missed badly as enterprise customers consolidated workloads onto fewer platforms.

Results were mixed across the semiconductor equipment sector: ASML raised guidance on strong EUV demand while Lam Research and Applied Materials both warned of headwinds from China export restrictions.

The market reacted negatively to news that the social media platform's founder had sold 4 billion dollars of company shares over the past three months, raising questions about insider confidence in the growth outlook.

Shares of the legacy automaker fell after its electric vehicle division reported an operating loss of 4,000 dollars per vehicle, far worse than investor expectations, while its combustion engine unit continued to generate strong cash flows.
The regional bank reported a sharp increase in commercial real estate loan losses, triggering a broader selloff in smaller US lenders as investors feared contagion across the sector.

The pharmaceutical company's blockbuster drug lost patent protection, with generic versions from Teva Pharmaceutical and Mylan immediately capturing over 80 percent of prescriptions within the first month.
The index fund giant disclosed it had voted against the re-election of board members at over 500 companies for failing to meet its minimum standards on climate disclosure and board diversity.
The food delivery company agreed to acquire its smaller rival in an all-stock deal, giving the target's shareholders a 27 percent premium but diluting the acquirer's existing investors.

The hedge fund manager was charged by the SEC with insider trading after allegedly trading on non-public information obtained from a board member at a technology company ahead of its acquisition announcement.

The central bank raised interest rates by 25 basis points for the eleventh consecutive time, sending shares of rate-sensitive sectors including real estate investment trusts and utilities sharply lower.
Shares of the defense contractor surged after the Pentagon awarded it a 13 billion dollar contract to develop next-generation hypersonic missile systems, beating out its longtime rival for the award.
The asset manager reported record inflows into its fixed income ETFs as investors rotated out of equity funds, reflecting growing risk aversion among retail and institutional clients alike.
The insurance giant raised its catastrophe loss estimate for the quarter to 3.2 billion dollars following a series of severe weather events, sending shares of reinsurers lower in sympathy.
The private equity firm completed the largest leveraged buyout of the year, acquiring the industrial conglomerate for 32 billion dollars after outbidding three rival consortia.
The sovereign wealth fund disclosed it had taken a 5 percent stake in the struggling luxury retailer, providing a lifeline for a company that had issued three profit warnings in the past year.
The ratings agency downgraded the country's sovereign debt to junk status, citing deteriorating fiscal metrics and a rising debt-to-GDP ratio that exceeded 120 percent.

The commodity trader was indicted on bribery charges in three jurisdictions simultaneously, with prosecutors alleging it paid over 100 million dollars in bribes to secure oil contracts in West Africa.
The logistics company filed for bankruptcy after its largest customer, which accounted for 58 percent of its revenue, terminated its contract with 30 days notice to bring operations in-house.
The cryptocurrency exchange emerged from Chapter 11 bankruptcy and began distributing 14.7 billion dollars to creditors, with most customers set to receive more than their original claim value.

The e-commerce enabler filed for bankruptcy protection listing 2.1 billion dollars in liabilities, a dramatic collapse for a company that had been valued at 7 billion dollars just two years earlier during the pandemic boom.
Apple reported record revenue in the fourth quarter of the year, driven by strong iPhone and services growth.
Microsoft posted earnings per share of 2.94 dollars for the third quarter, beating the market consensus by 12 percent.
Tesla delivered 435,000 vehicles in the first half of the year, missing its annual target by a wide margin.
Nvidia's data center revenue grew 400 percent year over year, far exceeding Wall Street expectations for the period.
Amazon reported free cash flow of 21 billion dollars over the trailing twelve months, a record for the company.
Meta's advertising revenue recovered strongly in the second half, with growth accelerating each month through December.
Google's operating margin expanded by 300 basis points in the latest quarter as headcount reductions took effect.
JPMorgan reported net interest income declined sequentially in the most recent quarter as deposit costs rose faster than loan yields.
Pfizer's revenue fell 42 percent in the full year as Covid vaccine and antiviral sales normalized to pre-pandemic levels.
Intel's data center division lost market share for the fifth consecutive quarter as enterprise customers accelerated their transition to cloud infrastructure.
Salesforce raised its full-year revenue guidance to between 34.7 and 34.8 billion dollars, citing strong demand across all product lines.
Boeing's commercial airplane deliveries fell short of its annual target for the third year in a row due to ongoing production constraints.
The Federal Reserve held interest rates steady for the third consecutive meeting, signaling a cautious approach to monetary easing.
Goldman Sachs reported its investment banking revenue rebounded strongly in the second quarter after two years of subdued deal activity.
Shopify's gross merchandise volume crossed one trillion dollars on an annualized basis for the first time in the company's history.
Netflix added 9.3 million subscribers globally in the most recent quarter, well ahead of its own guidance of 6 million.
Uber posted its strongest quarterly earnings since going public, with both its rides and delivery segments generating positive operating income.
TSMC's revenue grew 33 percent in the most recent month compared to the same period last year, driven entirely by AI chip demand.
Qualcomm's handset chip division returned to growth in the fiscal third quarter after four consecutive quarters of year-over-year declines.
Palantir raised its full-year revenue outlook for the fourth time this fiscal year as US commercial demand continued to accelerate beyond management expectations.
Intel failed to match the performance standards set by AMD and Nvidia in the data center market, losing ground in both server CPUs and AI accelerators.
Boeing's delivery numbers fell well short of the benchmark set by Airbus, which had posted its strongest quarterly output in five years.
Snap's user growth failed to keep pace with TikTok and Instagram Reels, which continued to dominate short-form video engagement among younger demographics.
Twitter's advertising revenue recovery lagged far behind Meta and Alphabet, both of which reported double-digit ad growth in the same period.
Lyft's ride volume growth trailed Uber by a wide margin, as the market leader continued to extend its dominance in both domestic and international markets.
Bing's search market share gains were modest compared to the explosive growth of Perplexity and ChatGPT search, which attracted millions of users away from traditional search engines.
Qualcomm's PC chip ambitions fell short of expectations set by Apple Silicon, which continued to dominate laptop performance benchmarks across every price tier.
AMD's server CPU market share gains were modest relative to the explosive growth Nvidia achieved in the same data center infrastructure budgets.
Walmart's e-commerce growth, while solid, continued to fall short of the pace set by Amazon, which widened its lead as the dominant online retailer.
Ford's EV sales failed to meet the targets set by Tesla and BYD, both of which reported record deliveries in the same quarter.
PayPal's user engagement metrics lagged the standards established by Block's Cash App and Apple Pay, which both reported accelerating transaction volumes.
Zoom's enterprise growth failed to match the momentum of Microsoft Teams and Google Meet, which had both deepened their integration into corporate workflows.
Dell's server revenue growth was underwhelming compared to the record figures posted by Super Micro Computer, which dominated AI server deployments.
Oracle's cloud infrastructure growth remained far behind the pace set by AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud, which collectively captured 65 percent of global cloud spending.
Peloton's connected fitness subscriber base continued to shrink in contrast to Apple Fitness Plus and Garmin, which reported steady growth in paid health and wellness subscribers.
Google filed a complaint against Apple with European regulators, alleging that Apple's App Store rules unfairly disadvantaged third-party browser applications.
Samsung filed a patent infringement suit against Ericsson over wireless communication technology used in its base station equipment.
Qualcomm filed an antitrust complaint against Arm Holdings, alleging that Arm had improperly terminated its architecture license in retaliation for a business dispute.
Epic Games filed a second antitrust lawsuit against Apple, this time targeting its App Store commission structure in European markets following the Digital Markets Act.
Spotify filed a formal complaint against Apple with the European Commission over its App Store payment requirements, calling the rules anticompetitive.
Netflix sued a former employee for allegedly stealing proprietary recommendation algorithm code and sharing it with a competitor.
Nvidia filed patent infringement claims against several Chinese chip startups it alleged had copied its GPU architecture for use in AI accelerator products.
Tesla won its lawsuit against a former employee who had leaked confidential Autopilot source code to a Chinese electric vehicle startup.
Microsoft won its long-running patent dispute against Motorola over video codec technology used in Xbox and Windows products.
Apple won a significant ruling against Masimo in a patent dispute over blood oxygen sensing technology used in its smartwatch products.
Broadcom won its patent infringement case against Qualcomm, with the jury awarding 1.1 billion dollars in damages for unauthorized use of its wireless chip technology.
InterDigital won a patent royalty case against Xiaomi in a UK court, establishing a global licensing rate for its wireless communication patents.
Amazon lost a labor dispute ruling in the European Court of Justice, which found that its warehouse monitoring practices violated worker privacy rights.
Google lost its appeal against a 4.1 billion euro EU antitrust fine related to Android bundling practices, with the court upholding the original penalty in full.
Apple lost a patent case brought by Ericsson over wireless standard essential patents, with the court ordering it to pay royalties on all iPhone sales globally.
Meta lost a data privacy case in Germany, with the court ruling that its behavioral advertising practices required explicit user consent under GDPR.
Uber lost a landmark employment tribunal case in the UK, with judges ruling that its drivers were workers entitled to minimum wage and holiday pay.
Qualcomm lost its antitrust appeal in the United States, with the court ruling that its no-license-no-chips policy constituted illegal monopolistic behavior.
Alphabet reported strong quarterly earnings, with Google Search revenue growing 14 percent. Its shares rose 7 percent in after-hours trading as investors cheered the results.
Tesla reported a sharp decline in automotive gross margins. Its stock fell 9 percent as investors worried the aggressive price cuts were eroding profitability faster than volume gains could offset.
Nvidia posted record data center revenue of 22.6 billion dollars. Its shares surged 10 percent after management raised guidance for the next quarter, citing insatiable demand for its AI chips.
Meta reported advertising revenue grew 27 percent year over year. Its stock climbed 15 percent as the results far exceeded analyst expectations and the company announced a first-ever dividend.
Boeing disclosed a 1.3 billion dollar charge related to its 737 MAX program. Its shares fell 4 percent as the writedown raised fresh concerns about the program's long-term profitability.
Amazon Web Services reported 17 percent revenue growth, beating estimates. Its operating margin expanded 400 basis points as efficiency initiatives took hold across the cloud division.
Apple's services segment reported 14 percent revenue growth. Its gross margin reached 74 percent, the highest level in the division's history, driven by App Store and subscription growth.

Intel announced a new manufacturing process node that it claimed would close the gap with TSMC. Its shares rose 6 percent as investors welcomed signs that the company's turnaround was gaining traction.

Salesforce reported weaker than expected revenue growth. Its stock dropped 20 percent — the largest single-day decline in two years — as management cited elongating sales cycles and cautious enterprise spending.
Shopify reported gross merchandise volume grew 24 percent. Its shares jumped 18 percent after management said it would divest its logistics business to focus on its core commerce platform.
Microsoft reported Azure grew 29 percent. Its overall revenue beat estimates across all three segments as AI-powered products drove faster renewal rates in its enterprise customer base.
Qualcomm reported handset chip revenue grew 12 percent. Its automotive chip division reached a record quarterly revenue of 899 million dollars, emerging as a key growth driver for the company.
Pfizer reported a 41 percent revenue decline in the full year. Its shares fell 6 percent as management cut the dividend for the first time in decades to preserve cash for pipeline investments.

The results were mixed across the social media sector. While Meta reported strong advertising growth of 27 percent, Pinterest missed estimates and Snap warned of slowing demand from brand advertisers.
Semiconductor stocks diverged sharply after earnings. Nvidia surged on record AI chip demand while Texas Instruments fell after warning of a prolonged inventory correction in industrial markets.

Cloud stocks moved in opposite directions following their quarterly reports. Microsoft and Google both beat estimates on strong AI adoption while Oracle disappointed with slower than expected cloud migration revenue.

EV makers reported contrasting fortunes this quarter. Tesla missed delivery estimates while Rivian exceeded its production guidance and BYD posted record monthly sales for the third consecutive month.
Drug makers split after trial results. Eli Lilly surged after its obesity pill matched injectable results while Pfizer dropped after its competing weight loss drug showed a weaker efficacy profile.

Streaming results were broadly positive. Netflix, Disney Plus, and Paramount Plus all reported subscriber gains that exceeded analyst forecasts, suggesting the market was large enough to support multiple platforms.
Retail earnings painted a divided picture. Costco and Walmart beat on same-store sales while Target and Kohl's both missed estimates as lower-income consumers pulled back on discretionary spending.
Airline results diverged by strategy. Delta and United outperformed on strong premium cabin demand while Southwest struggled as its point-to-point model faced rising cost pressure.

Bank earnings were broadly better than feared. JPMorgan and Wells Fargo both beat net interest income forecasts while regional lenders including KeyCorp and Regions Financial missed on higher credit loss provisions.
Also weighing on sentiment was news that Qualcomm had lost a key design win at Samsung to MediaTek, which had offered more competitive pricing for its mid-range Dimensity processors.
Shares of Arm fell sharply after Qualcomm announced it had developed its own CPU cores, reducing its reliance on Arm's standard designs for future laptop and server chips.

Also notable was the announcement that Apple had dropped Broadcom as a supplier for certain wireless components, switching to its own in-house design in a move that could cost Broadcom several hundred million dollars annually.
Concerns mounted after reports emerged that TSMC had begun diverting capacity previously reserved for Intel Foundry customers to fill surging orders from Nvidia and Apple.
Adding to the pressure on legacy automakers was confirmation that BYD had overtaken Volkswagen as the best-selling car brand in China, a market Volkswagen had dominated for two decades.
Investor concern grew after it emerged that three senior engineers from Apple's Vision Pro team had quietly joined Meta's augmented reality division over the past two months.
The situation at Intel worsened after its newly appointed CEO announced the departure of four division heads within his first ninety days, raising questions about strategic direction.
OpenAI strengthened its safety team by hiring three prominent AI researchers from DeepMind who had co-authored key papers on alignment and interpretability.
Anthropic appointed a former Meta infrastructure engineer as its new chief technology officer, signaling a shift toward scaling compute operations ahead of its next model release.
Rivian lost its head of manufacturing to Tesla just weeks before its new plant was scheduled to begin production, creating uncertainty about the launch timeline.
Stripe hired the former CFO of Klarna ahead of its anticipated IPO, bringing in senior financial leadership with direct experience navigating public market transitions.
Google Cloud poached the head of AWS's financial services division along with a team of eight enterprise account managers, in a targeted move to accelerate its penetration of the banking sector.
Palantir lost two of its founding engineers to a new AI startup backed by Andreessen Horowitz, with the departures seen as a signal of internal frustration with the company's direction.
Apple launched its first generative AI features across iPhone, iPad, and Mac, positioning Apple Intelligence as a privacy-first alternative to ChatGPT and Google Gemini.
Google released its Gemini 2.0 model family, claiming it outperformed Claude 3.5 and GPT-4o on the majority of standard benchmarks.
Samsung launched its Galaxy Ring, entering the wearable health monitoring market previously dominated by Oura Ring and Garmin's fitness trackers.
Waymo launched its robotaxi service in a fourth US city, extending its lead over Cruise, which remained suspended pending regulatory review.
Mistral AI released Le Chat, a consumer-facing assistant it positioned as a European privacy-respecting alternative to ChatGPT and Claude.
Arm unveiled its latest Cortex-X925 CPU core, claiming a 40 percent performance improvement over its predecessor and putting direct pressure on Intel and AMD in the laptop market.
Rivian launched its R2 SUV at a starting price of 45,000 dollars, targeting the mass market segment currently dominated by Tesla Model Y and Ford Mustang Mach-E.
Broadcom released a new custom AI chip architecture for hyperscale customers that it claimed delivered twice the performance per watt of Nvidia's competing solution.
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company broke ground on its second Arizona fab, a milestone that underscored the momentum of the US CHIPS Act in reshoring semiconductor production.
Exxon Mobil announced a 20 billion dollar share buyback, reinforcing its commitment to capital returns despite calls from activist investors to accelerate the energy transition.
Blackstone raised its largest real estate fund ever at 30.4 billion dollars, demonstrating that institutional appetite for alternative assets remained robust despite rising interest rates.
Volkswagen announced it would shut two factories in Germany and cut 35,000 jobs as it struggled to compete with cheaper Chinese electric vehicles flooding European markets.
Novartis completed the spinoff of its generics division Sandoz, creating two independently listed companies with distinct strategic mandates and investor bases.
Softbank invested 500 million dollars in Perplexity AI at a valuation of 9 billion dollars, making it one of the most heavily backed AI search startups globally.
Comcast announced it would spin off its cable television network assets into a separate public company, freeing its broadband and theme park businesses from the declining linear TV business.
Honda and Nissan announced a merger to create the third largest automaker in the world by volume, combining their complementary strengths in hybrid and electric vehicle technology.
Synopsys terminated its proposed acquisition of Ansys after failing to obtain regulatory approval in China, writing off 18 months of merger integration planning.
Adobe abandoned its 20 billion dollar acquisition of Figma after regulators in the UK and EU blocked the deal, citing concerns about competition in the design software market.
The SEC charged the founder of a crypto exchange with securities fraud, alleging he had misled investors about the platform's risk controls and used customer funds for personal investments.
The FTC sued a major pharmacy benefit manager, alleging it had inflated insulin prices through anticompetitive rebate arrangements that harmed patients and independent pharmacies.
The DOJ indicted a former Goldman Sachs banker for his role in the 1MDB scandal, alleging he had facilitated the theft of billions of dollars from the Malaysian sovereign wealth fund.
The CFPB ruled that Buy Now Pay Later products must offer the same consumer protections as credit cards, a decision that put significant regulatory pressure on Affirm, Klarna, and Afterpay.
Nvidia was subpoenaed by the DOJ as part of an antitrust investigation into whether it had used its dominant market position in AI chips to stifle competition from smaller rivals.
Apple was ordered by a US appeals court to allow third-party payment links in its App Store, a ruling that could cost it several billion dollars in annual commission revenue.
Meta was fined 1.3 billion euros by Irish regulators for transferring European user data to US servers in violation of GDPR, the largest data protection fine ever imposed.
Binance was alleged by the CFTC of operating an illegal derivatives exchange and deliberately evading US compliance requirements through a complex web of offshore entities.
SAP agreed to pay 220 million dollars to settle DOJ allegations that it had paid bribes to government officials in multiple countries to secure software contracts.
Coinbase was authorized by the SEC to operate as a registered national securities exchange, resolving years of regulatory uncertainty and establishing a clear legal framework for its operations.
Roche received FDA approval for its Alzheimer's diagnostic test, enabling earlier detection of amyloid plaques and opening a new revenue stream ahead of the launch of its treatment pipeline.
Moderna was authorized by European regulators to market its RSV vaccine for adults over 60, entering a market previously dominated by Pfizer and GSK.
Spirit Airlines filed for bankruptcy protection after its merger with Frontier Airlines fell through and a proposed acquisition by JetBlue was blocked by regulators.
Red Lobster filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after years of declining foot traffic and a disastrous endless shrimp promotion that cost the restaurant chain an estimated 11 million dollars.
WeWork filed for bankruptcy after years of mounting losses and a failed IPO attempt, marking one of the most dramatic collapses of a venture-backed startup in recent memory.
Rite Aid filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection, weighed down by opioid litigation settlements and a shrinking retail pharmacy business facing intense competition from CVS and Walgreens.
Tupperware filed for bankruptcy after failing to refinance its debt load and losing relevance with younger consumers who had shifted to alternative food storage brands.
Lordstown Motors filed for bankruptcy and sued Foxconn for allegedly backing out of a deal to acquire its Ohio electric truck manufacturing plant.
Bed Bath and Beyond emerged from bankruptcy as a purely online retailer, shedding its physical store network entirely and relaunching under new ownership with a digital-only model.
Hertz exited bankruptcy and relisted on Nasdaq, raising 1.3 billion dollars in a successful IPO that gave the rental car company a fresh financial foundation for its fleet electrification strategy.
Diamond Sports Group emerged from bankruptcy after reaching a restructuring agreement with its regional sports network partners and a subset of its major league team clients.
Celsius Network exited bankruptcy and began distributing recovered assets to creditors, returning approximately 67 cents on the dollar to customers who had deposited funds on the platform.
OpenAI and Microsoft expanded their partnership with an additional 10 billion dollar investment, deepening the integration of GPT-4 models across Microsoft's enterprise product suite.
Apple and TSMC signed a long-term supply agreement securing priority access to the most advanced 2-nanometer process nodes for iPhone and Mac chips through 2030.
Sony and Honda launched their Afeela electric vehicle brand as a joint venture, combining Sony's entertainment technology with Honda's automotive manufacturing expertise.
Pfizer and BioNTech announced a collaboration to develop mRNA-based cancer vaccines targeting multiple solid tumor types, expanding their successful COVID vaccine partnership.
Amazon and Anthropic deepened their AI partnership with Amazon committing an additional 2.75 billion dollars and Anthropic agreeing to run its most powerful models exclusively on AWS infrastructure.
Google and Samsung extended their cross-licensing agreement covering mobile technology patents, avoiding a costly legal dispute that had threatened to disrupt their longstanding partnership.

Uber and Waymo launched a commercial partnership allowing Waymo's autonomous vehicles to be hailed through the Uber app in select cities, marking the first major integration of robotaxis into a mainstream ride-hailing platform.

Netflix and WWE signed a landmark 5-billion-dollar deal making Netflix the exclusive global home of Monday Night Raw, marking the wrestling franchise's first move to streaming after decades on traditional cable.
The chipmaker's shares fell 12 percent after it warned that data center customers were pausing orders to reassess their AI infrastructure needs following a period of aggressive overbuilding.
The social media company's stock dropped 8 percent after its chief executive acknowledged that younger users were spending less time on the platform than in previous years.
The electric vehicle maker surged 15 percent after its founder announced a surprise production record, sending short sellers scrambling to cover positions built up over the prior weeks.

The streaming giant gained 9 percent after raising its subscription prices for the second time in eighteen months, with analysts noting the increases had not triggered meaningful churn in its subscriber base.
The German automaker dropped 7 percent after revealing it would miss its electric vehicle sales targets for the second consecutive year, blaming weak consumer demand across European markets.

The pharmaceutical giant gained 11 percent after its blockbuster weight loss drug posted stronger than expected trial results in a new cardiovascular indication, significantly expanding its potential patient population.

The search giant's shares fell 5 percent as investors worried that AI-powered answer engines were beginning to erode its core advertising business by reducing the number of clicks through to commercial websites.
The cloud computing provider surged 13 percent after its chief financial officer said demand from AI workloads was accelerating faster than the company could build new data center capacity.
Musk's rocket company secured a 2.9 billion dollar NASA contract for its Starship vehicle to serve as the lunar lander for the Artemis program's crewed moon mission.
Zuckerberg's social media empire reported its strongest revenue quarter since going public, driven by a recovery in digital advertising and accelerating growth from its Reels short video product.

Cook's company unveiled its most ambitious product lineup in a decade, with analysts predicting the new devices could drive an unprecedented upgrade cycle among its installed base of over two billion active users.
Huang's chipmaker reached a market capitalization of 3 trillion dollars, briefly making it the most valuable public company in the world ahead of Microsoft and Apple.
Bezos's e-commerce giant announced it would invest 100 billion dollars in US data center infrastructure over the next five years, the largest single capital commitment in the company's history.
The iPhone maker's shares rose 4 percent after a supply chain report confirmed it had begun mass production of its next generation headset, easing investor concerns about a delayed launch.
The Windows maker gained 3 percent after a regulatory filing revealed that Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway had initiated a new position in the company during the prior quarter.
The GPU maker's stock slipped 3 percent as traders took profits following a 200 percent rally over the prior twelve months, even as the company's fundamental outlook remained unchanged.
Amazon's logistics arm undercut FedEx and UPS on same-day delivery pricing, triggering a sharp selloff in both legacy carriers as investors reassessed their competitive positioning.
Tesla's insurance product gained traction in three new US states, putting it in direct competition with Allstate and Progressive in the usage-based auto insurance market.
Apple's credit card division expanded its high-yield savings account product to all fifty US states, increasing competitive pressure on Marcus by Goldman Sachs and Ally Financial.

Google's Waymo unit secured operating licenses in two additional US cities over the same week that Cruise remained grounded following its regulatory suspension, widening the gap between the two autonomous vehicle programs.
Microsoft's GitHub Copilot reached 1.8 million paid subscribers, putting significant pressure on JetBrains and other developer tools vendors who had not yet launched comparable AI coding assistants.

Nvidia's DGX Cloud service attracted ten new enterprise customers in a single quarter, drawing contracts away from established cloud providers including IBM and Oracle who had targeted the same accounts.

Apple's App Store generated 89 billion dollars in developer billings, reinforcing its position as the world's most profitable software distribution platform and putting distance between itself and Google Play.

The results were a clear vindication for Arm's licensing model. Its royalty revenue surged 37 percent as virtually every major chip designer — from Apple to Qualcomm to MediaTek — continued to increase their reliance on its CPU and GPU architectures.
The situation underscored the broader challenge facing legacy media. While Warner Bros Discovery and Paramount both reported subscriber losses, Netflix added 9 million new users in the same period.

The divergence in AI investment highlighted contrasting strategies across the sector. While Salesforce and ServiceNow reported strong AI-driven upsells, SAP warned its customers were taking longer to adopt its new AI modules.

The contrasting fortunes were stark. Shopify reported accelerating growth in its merchant solutions segment while BigCommerce warned of slowing enterprise customer additions and cut its full-year revenue forecast.
The semiconductor equipment sector split after earnings. ASML beat estimates on strong EUV tool demand while Lam Research and Applied Materials both missed on weaker memory chip spending.

Analyst sentiment toward the cybersecurity sector shifted sharply after CrowdStrike raised guidance while Palo Alto Networks cut its billings forecast, citing a decision to move customers to a new consumption-based pricing model.

Delivery platforms diverged in their latest quarter. DoorDash gained market share in suburban markets while Instacart struggled to retain grocery delivery customers who had returned to in-store shopping.
The fintech landscape showed clear winners and losers. Block's Cash App reported record gross profit while PayPal struggled to articulate a clear AI strategy to skeptical investors.
Space launch results were mixed. SpaceX completed its 100th mission of the year ahead of schedule while Rocket Lab reported a launch failure that set back its small satellite deployment business.

Nvidia hired over 200 AI researchers from academic institutions and startups in a single quarter, accelerating its push to build the largest in-house AI research organization in the semiconductor industry.
Apple hired a team of former Google Brain researchers specializing in on-device AI inference optimization, signaling a major push to run more powerful AI models locally on its hardware.

Meta hired former US Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency director as its new head of fundamental AI research, a high-profile appointment that signaled its ambitions to lead in AGI-adjacent research.
Tesla lost three senior engineers from its Full Self-Driving team within a single month, with two joining rival Waymo and one founding a new autonomous vehicle startup.
Amazon Web Services hired the former head of Google Cloud's financial services vertical, targeting enterprise banking clients that AWS had previously struggled to win away from its rival.
Qualcomm poached Samsung's chief mobile chip architect, a move widely seen as an attempt to accelerate development of its next generation flagship smartphone processor.
OpenAI lost its chief safety officer and head of alignment research within the same week, triggering a public debate about whether safety was being deprioritized in favor of faster product releases.
Anthropic hired a team of former OpenAI engineers who had worked on GPT-4 post-training, strengthening its ability to compete at the frontier of large language model development.
Intel named a former Qualcomm executive as its new chief product officer, bringing in leadership with a track record of successful chip launches in mobile and automotive markets.
Microsoft laid off 1,900 employees from its gaming division following the completion of its Activision Blizzard acquisition, consolidating overlapping functions across the enlarged games business.

Samsung filed for regulatory approval to acquire a controlling stake in ASML, a move that would give the South Korean conglomerate preferential access to the world's most advanced chip manufacturing equipment.
Chevron announced a takeover bid for Hess Corporation at a valuation of 53 billion dollars, seeking to expand its deepwater oil reserves in Guyana ahead of an anticipated energy supply gap.

Cisco announced a 28 billion dollar acquisition of Splunk, its largest deal ever, aimed at transforming itself from a networking hardware company into an AI-powered security and observability platform.

Mastercard announced it would acquire a payments infrastructure startup for 3.2 billion dollars, adding real-time account-to-account payment capabilities that would allow it to compete more directly with Visa and PayPal.
Alphabet announced it would acquire cybersecurity firm Wiz for 23 billion dollars, its largest acquisition ever, bolstering Google Cloud's security capabilities against Microsoft Azure and AWS.

Broadcom completed its 69 billion dollar acquisition of VMware, creating a combined enterprise software and semiconductor company that analysts said would reshape the data center infrastructure market.
Elon Musk's acquisition of Twitter closed at 44 billion dollars, triggering an immediate exodus of advertisers and senior engineers who objected to his planned changes to content moderation policy.
Amazon canceled its planned acquisition of iRobot after EU regulators signaled they would block the deal, leaving the Roomba maker without a buyer and forcing it to announce significant layoffs.

Nvidia launched its Blackwell GPU architecture, claiming a fourfold improvement in AI training performance over its predecessor Hopper, putting Intel and AMD on notice that the performance gap had widened further.
Apple unveiled its M4 chip family, delivering significant performance and efficiency gains that the company said would power its AI features without requiring cloud processing.
AMD released its Instinct MI300X AI accelerator, citing benchmark results that it claimed matched or exceeded Nvidia's H100 in memory bandwidth and large model inference throughput.
Anthropic released Claude 3.5 Sonnet, claiming it outperformed GPT-4o on coding and reasoning benchmarks, marking the first time an Anthropic model had topped the major leaderboards.
